other_observers: Ted Floyd, Norm Erthal, Joe Roller, Alison Hilf, about 15 others on CFO field trip;
specific_location: Fox Ranch, Yuma County Road U at the Arikaree River, a few hundred meters east of the ranch headquarters, south of the river and just above the floodplain.
description: Quite small sparrow, noticeably smaller than nearby Vesper Sparrows. Top-heavy, large-headed, flat-crowned, short-tailed structure of ammodramus sparrows. The most distinctive feature, seen as the bird perched facing me for several seconds, was a clean-cut orange breast with very faint dark streaking and a horizontal lower border separating it from a clean white belly. One member of our group, unfamiliar with this species, compared this to a Pectoral Sandpiper's breast (referring to the clean, abrupt lower border.) Orange was also visible on the face, with a gray auricular and dark eye-line, and dark crown creating the facial pattern when the bird turned its head sideways. The most anterior part of the blue-gray nape was visible as its head was briefly turned sideways. The bill was moderately long and fairly sharp for a sparrow. I didn't get a clear side view, but when seen at close range flying away I had the brief impression of a bluish-gray nape, dark back, orange-brown rump, and short, wiry tail. I had this view about 4 times.Behaviors: The bird popped up onto the top of a weed stalk as our large group approached, facing us. It flushed into thicker grass and weeds, where it held tight as we circled and slowly closed in on it, flushing only as someone got within a few meters of it. It repeated this about three more times before flying to much taller, more dense grass along the floodplain of the Arikaree river where we could not pursue it.Call: Not HeardPlumage: Adult nelsoni
